content:
I have mixed feelings about this puzzle. It was very fun, but I feel the step-by-step guide is really spoonfeeding the user, especially in computing theta (maybe you can use the "trigonometry" tag  and cut that part, simply specifying that you want the result in degrees and ranging from 0 to 360).

That's also because there are other possible methods to compute such angle. Some languages (like python) even support complex numbers, so the problem just comes down to sum up every force and compute the absolute value and the phase of the resulting force. My mixed feelings come straight from that: while it's been both easy and fun solving it that way, that still required some basic trigonometry knowledge. Can we ask that from a programmer?

I mean: if we don't require basic trigonometry, then such a detailed guide is totally needed and expected. Otherwise, it's simply too much detailed, leaving no space for the user to come up with a solution on their own.

I don't have a real (no pun intended) answer for that. I'm still upvoting the contribution because it was fun, good work :)

Hi! Nice puzzle. In addition to the comments mentioned by 5DN1L, it might also be helpful to clearly specify the desired rounding method (E.g. “Round half up"), after "corrected to two decimal places".

Goal
* In the line before the example, please add a period after "[[theta]]".

* Please add a blank line before the example.

* In sqrt[10^2+(-25)^2], please swap -25 and 10 so that the components appear in x-y order.

Output
* Line 1 & Line 2: Please replace "resultant" with "balancing".

* Line 2: Please state that the angle is measured in degrees.

Constraints
* Please add the range of possible values for [[n]] as the first constraint.

* Please rewrite the second constraint to explicitly show the range of possible values for [[F]]. After that, mention that [[F]] is an integer in the Input section.

Stub generator
* Please change "force" to "f" for consistency with other sections.

* Please add a write statement, e.g. write answer.

Cases
* Already in Equilibrium (x-direction) (Test 2): Please remove the trailing newline from the output.

* Please add a pair of cases where [[n]] = 1 before the more complex cases.

* Currently, the cases use values of [[n]] that are either very small or very large. Also, some angle ranges for the balancing force are not covered:
  • Tests: 180 < theta < 270, 270 < theta < 360
  • Validators: 0 < theta < 90
  Please add cases with intermediate values of [[n]] (e.g. one pair with [[n]] around 50, and another pair with [[n]] around 500) to cover these scenarios.

Cover picture
* Consider adding a cover picture for the puzzle.

Thanks.
Thank you for your feedback. I will implement the suggestions above when I’m free!
Thank you for your input! I could add an example to demonstrate what “corrected to two decimal places” mean. Does that work?

Thank you for such a detailed feedback. 

The reason why I included such a detailed guide as I thought this is university level mechanics problem and coders might not be familiar with the maths behind it. I do understand your concern though as this is a puzzle and coders can do some research on this. 

I will give that a thought and see what I can come up with to encourage coders to code their own solution. 

Thank you once again!

Why not. However, to ensure players don't get frustrated by floating-point issues, I would suggest being even more specific.
"corrected to two decimal places" can be interpreted differently across languages (e.g. Python's round() behaves differently than JavaScript's Math.round() for values ending in .005)... Hence my proposition to explicitly stating the rounding method.
How about “round to the nearest hundredth” with an example? That basically means rounding half up.

Well, to be 100% sure, just check that your current test cases don't contain any theta values ending too close to '.XX5'. If that's not the case, that's fine with me :)

EDIT: I've tested the puzzle across some languages but not all (Python, C#, Ruby, and Js, and all the test cases looks ok though. So I won't argue any further! :) Great job!
All changes are implemented. Would you please take a look again and see if there is anything else that needs to be changed? Thank you!

Sure! Below are the comments on the updated contribution.

Input
* Please add "(integer)" after the first "([[F]])".

* Please add "(string)" after "[[dir]]".

* Please delete the last sentence.

Constraints
* Please move the constraint on [[dir]] to the end of the section so that the constraints follow the same order as the inputs.

* Please delete "[[n]] is a positive integer;".

* Please delete "[[F]] is a positive integer;".

Stub generator
* Please delete the second "write" keyword; "theta" alone is sufficient since "write" accepts a multi-line parameter.

Cases
* Already in Equilibrium (y-direction) (Test 3): Please reduce 6223542 to a value below 100 so that it stays within the constraint and the case aligns with Validator 3. (Note: I also don't see a reason for using values greater than 1000000. This note also applies to the relevant points below.)

* Validator 5: Please increase 70 and 40 to 3-digit integers to align with Negative x-direction (Test 5).

* Validator 6: Please reduce 4654313 and 2315464 to 4-digit integers so that they stay within the constraint.

* Second Quadrant (Test 12): Please reduce 1001110 so that it stays within the constraint.

* Validator 12: Please include some 5-digit integers to align with Test 12.

* Validators 13 and 16 are missing. Please renumber the cases accordingly.

* "Validator 15" (counterpart to Test 14): Please reduce 6209409 and 6305918 so that they stay within the constraint.

Cover picture
* Consider adding a cover picture for the puzzle.

Thanks.

Should all be fixed except for the cover picture (still finding a decent picture lol)

thanks once again!
